In Yoshi's flashback, Yoshi gets in trouble after accidentally knocking over the skeleton of a Tyrannosaurus rex in a Brooklyn museum. He would later end up getting into trouble with a real live T. rex and then facing a real undead reptilian skeleton . Early in the film, while Luigi is convincing Mario to ask Peach out, he makes a note of asking him to ask her if she has a friend he could hook up with. While his doesn't come to pass within the movie itself, it does hint at the second Stinger revealing Princess Daisy, Luigi's Implied Love Interest in the games . After Mario calls Bowser's painting "trash", Bowser gets enraged and threatens Mario, saying he'll "burn the skin off [Mario's] bones". Guess what happens to Bowser in the final battle? During the tour of Planet Bowser, one of the rides has several Koopa Troopas riding a roller coaster. The ride dunks them into lava, where they harmlessly come out as Dry Bones. For those who may not be versed in Dry Bowser lore, this hints that Bowser falling into lava wouldn't kill him but rather turn him into an undead skeleton . Speaking of which, Bowser at one point paints a picture of Mario as a skeleton. Bowser himself would become a skeleton in the form of Dry Bowser when Mario drops him in a lava pit during the climax. Freeze-Frame Bonus : The fight between Mario and Bowser Jr.'s Wonder form has the former use a book to trap Bowser Jr.'s paintbrush axe, and the page shown right before it happens is a recipe that has a Theet decoration on it. Two ships that are in the background in the flight deck are the Falcon Flyer and Samus's gunship . The Mario Bros.' NES has made it to their house in the Mushroom Kingdom, and it looks like they've been playing Excitebike recently. Friendly Enemy : Bowser is on better terms with Luigi than Mario this time around, greeting Luigi more sincerely and Luigi complimenting Bowser's painting, which Mario calls "trash". In fact, after regaining his size, he actually helps them , where he willingly let himself stay on the Honeyhive Galaxy to work out the damage they unintentionally caused while the two brothers and Yoshi are sent to find Princess Peach at the Gateway Galaxy. Sadly, he decides to return to his villainous ways for his son's sake and any real friendliness he showed for the Bros. before pretty much dies during the climax of the film. Furry Confusion : During Yoshi’s flashback of him in Brooklyn, he goes into a museum and accidentally knocks down the skeleton of a T. rex . Later, while stranded in Fossil Falls, Yoshi, a cartoony, semi-anthropomorphic dinosaur, encounters... the realistic and non-anthropomorphic Tyrannosaurus rex that first appeared in Super Mario Odyssey . Getting Eaten Is Harmless : Three different occasions by three different characters, no less: The first is Kamek getting eaten by Yoshi, popping out of him as an egg. Kamek later emerges from the egg no worse for wear. The second is Baby Mario venturing into the alien T-Rex 's mouth. Granted, he (probably) never got further down than that and he was only in there for a couple of seconds, but a dinosaur's mouth likely isn't very pleasant either. The last one is Bowser Jr. being eaten by the Ruined Dragon he painted. Pretty justified since it was just made out of ink and not intended to have a digestive system. Giant Mook : A Big Cheep Cheep appears eating a Blue Toad. It takes Luigi in a Frog Suit saving that Toad by dive kicking the creature. Big Goombas also appear in the movie, where they are fought by Mario, Luigi, and Yoshi at a location that resembles both Giant Land from their game debut and Supermassive Galaxy from Super Mario Galaxy 2 . Going Native : Hilariously, Yoshi's flashback reveals that after being born on Earth, he travelled around and acted no different from a tourist. Visiting different places while adjusting to the city rapidly with no intent in returning home... until he caused a dinosaur skeleton to collapse at the Museum of Natural History and was forced to run away or be jailed. Go-Karting with Bowser : Deconstructed. Bowser is trying to rehabilitate himself, and he does so by attempting to befriend the Mario Bros. with painting showcases and book club sessions. Luigi is convinced by Bowser's progress and is open to lighten Bowser's sentence (namely letting him grow big). Mario, however, believes Bowser is faking his friendliness so he can then escape and get his revenge on the brothers for defeating him last time. It turns out that Bowser is genuinely trying to reform, as he helps the brothers out even after becoming big again. But Bowser Jr. finds his dad's softness on his former enemies to be a weakness and so he convinces his father that friends will eventually backstab him while family is forever. Bowser ends up sliding back into a villain and attempts to kill Mario, Luigi, and Peach for his son's happiness . Good Is Not Soft : Due to the events of the previous movie ( especially the ones about endangering Luigi ), Mario holds a very visible grudge over Bowser, and isn't hesitant to show his contempt for him by doing things like insult his painting as seen in the first trailer. Good Parents : Bowser is evil and goes back to Big Bad status again, but he is very much a good parent to his son, who loves and adores Bowser in turn and wants to be just as evil. The Greatest Story Never Told : Played for Laughs . Yoshi is more than happy to recount his tale of living in New York before coming back to the Mushroom Kingdom, but since he only speaks Yoshi, the audience sees it while Mario and Luigi are clueless. Mario: I guess we'll never know. Greater-Scope Villain : The unseen great evil that wanted Peach and Rosalina's powers, resulting in Rosalina sending Peach far away to the Mushroom Kingdom. Green-Eyed Monster : Toad is a bit jealous of the newly added Yoshi upon being introduced to him by Mario and Luigi. In the Japanese version, he's specifically jealous of how Mario and Luigi seem to think he's cuter, with cute being an important part of the Japanese movie Toads' culture. Hand Signals : Mario attempts to use an elaborate set of hand signals to communicate that he and Peach will go one way, while Yoshi and Luigi should go another. Luigi doesn't understand it at all. Harmless Freezing : During the "On Patrol" Montage of fixing the Mushroom Kingdom's problems, Ice Mario fist-bumps Luigi but accidentally freezes him in a block of ice in the process after fixing a Toad's ice cream. Hard Truth Aesop : People have the capacity to change, but can just refuse to. Both Mario Bros. give Bowser the benefit of the doubt and even Bowser hesitated over his son wanting to murder the brothers, but eventually (thanks to his son's influence), he decides to fully revert to being a villain. Hates Reading : It’s implied Bowser might hate reading, since his tirade against Peach in the climax has him mention being forced to join a book club with Luigi as one of the things he’s mad at her for . Heel–Face Revolving Door : Bowser appears to have reformed during his time as Peach's prisoner, and genuinely grows to respect the Mario Bros. throughout their adventure. Unfortunately, however, his son's Toxic Family Influence ends up causing him to go back to being evil in the climax. And unlike other examples of this trope, it is highly unlikely that he will try to go back and be good and a better person ever again, not only because he actively doesn't want to anymore , but by this point, he has already gone well past that point . Held Gaze : Like in the first film, Mario and Peach exchange these of the romantic variety to show how much they've grown to care (maybe even love) each other. Heroic BSoD : When Peach first spots a poster of Rosalina at the Gateway Galaxy, seeing the space princess's face for the first time, she freezes up as she has a brief traumatic flashback of her as a baby being forced to be sent away by a younger Rosalina and is left shell shocked for a brief while, needing Toad to snap her out of it. Hidden Depths : For the cruel, tyrannical monster he is, Bowser is shown to be a very good painter, on par with his piano skills. He is also a part of a book club with Luigi. Hit Me, Dammit! : Bowser looks for ways for Mario to punch him so he can grow back to normal size. He eventually gets it when he calls Mario a "failure of a plumber" (triggering how Mario's father initially looked down on the brothers going out on their own) so he punches the Koopa King. Hoist by His Own Petard : During the climax, Bowser Jr. creates the Ruined Dragon with his paintbrush, but in the midst of doing his Pre-Asskicking One-Liner , he immediately gets eaten and has to be saved by Mario. Hope Spot : When Bowser Jr. transports the captive Rosalina to Planet Bowser and announces his intentions to use her power to destroy the universe as well as her Lumas, she angrily explodes her way out of her restraints and starts fighting her way through Jr.'s army of minions. But before she can escape entirely, Bowser Jr. uses his Magic Paintbrush to cover her in paint, immobilizing her and putting her into a much stronger cage. When Peach's castle crashes in the Honeyhive Galaxy, Mario, Luigi and Yoshi race to avoid getting crushed underneath. They seem to evade this fate, and breath a sigh of relief... just before the castle tilts in their direction. Horrifying the Horror : Lumalee is the warden of the prison Dry Bowser and Bowser Jr. got locked up in, and his fatalistic ramblings manage to make both villains scream in horror. Human Aliens : As it turns out, both Princess Peach and Rosalina, who are sisters in the series, are cosmic beings made of stardust who simply look human. Hypocritical Humor : Toad takes an immediate dislike to Yoshi, complaining about how Mario and Luigi are allowing a "dinosaur" to join "the group" despite not knowing him for long. Not only is Toad a talking mushroom humanoid, but he invited himself on Mario and Peach's quest in the first movie despite barely knowing Mario at the time. Iconic Attribute Adoption Moment : Mario gives Peach her signature parasol from the games as a birthday present, which she uses throughout the film. Interspecies Romance : Not quite full-on romance just yet, but by the end, Mario and Peach are clearly on their way to becoming a real item, and this was after it was revealed that Peach, alongside her sister, is a human alien made from stardust. In the Hood : During their visit to the Sand Kingdom, Mario and Luigi are seen wearing cloaks with goggles whilst in the desert, likely to protect themselves from the heat and sand rather than as a disguise. I Resemble That Remark! : When Mario tries to calm Peach down by acknowledging she's frustrated, Peach yells "Princesses don't get frustrated!" Jumping Fish : As the camera pans over a lake in the teaser, a school of Cheep-Cheeps leap in and out of the water. Just Here for Godzilla : In-Universe . After the Lumas practically beg Rosalina to read the story about the Mario Brothers, she barely gets a word in before they ask her to skip ahead to the part with Donkey Kong . Know When to Fold 'Em : Yoshi tries to frighten off a Tyrannosaurus rex by roaring at it. Unfortunately, the T. rex does a far more efficient roar that causes Yoshi and Toad (who's carrying Baby Mario and Baby Luigi) to run away in panic. Lady of War : Like in the last film, Peach once more shows off her fighting skills as she takes on some of Bowser and Wart's forces. The beginning of the movie also shows Rosalina single-handedly decimating a Megaleg using only her wand. Lampshade Hanging : Toad lampshades how Yoshi quickly joins the brothers and becomes part of the gang after his introduction. Wart takes a jab at how princesses seem to have a habit of getting kidnapped. Laser-Guided Karma : In the first movie, Bowser had captured Lumalee, whose imprisonment seems to have shaped her nihilistic personality. But as seen in The Stinger for this film, Lumalee has now become the guard at the prison where Bowser and his son have been imprisoned, and intends to make sure that they're the ones suffering now. Bowser attempted to dunk numerous innocents in cages in a pit of lava in the first movie. Here he ends up being dunked into a lava pit himself after Mario cut off the supports in self-defense. Late-Arrival Spoiler : The first official trailer opens with Bowser's still miniature size, which he was reduced to after the final battle in the first movie. Le Parkour : The "On Patrol" Montage has a brief sequence of Mario, Luigi and Yoshi jumping and vaulting their way past a pair of Lakitus in the Clouds, before jumping one by one into a Warp Pipe with the Blue Toad they rescued from the Big Cheep Cheep. Lighter and Softer : While Bowser Jr. has higher goals than his father did in the first film with larger stakes and comes close to killing Rosalina and the heroes a few times , the film is considerably lighter than the first being bereft of a major character arc for the protagonists who have moved past the self-doubt they struggled with in the first one. There are less pathos-driven scenes, less scenes of peril such as the attempted mass ritualistic sacrifice on Bowser's prisoners, and a much more adventurous plot and tone as a result. This is especially the case compared to the original Galaxy . Rosalina no longer has her original backstory or mother she had to accept was gone forever, instead being born from stardust and getting to happily reunite with her only living relative. The universe does not get destroyed and recreated either, with Bowser's attempt at doing so being prevented. The stakes are still higher than they were in Galaxy 2 , however. Living Battery : The main reason Bowser Jr. kidnaps Rosalina, to have her cosmic power be used as the power source for his doomsday weapon. Logo Joke : The Illumination logo has one of the Minions grab the hammer power-up from Donkey Kong (1981) , only to go on a rampage when another Minion plays the Hammer theme from that game, destroying the Illumination logo and smashing the latter into the ground in the process. The Nintendo logo features a small animation at the end commemorating the 40th anniversary of Super Mario Bros. 1 . invoked Loved by All : Rosalina is about to read a story about Peach, when an interrupting Luma demands to hear a story about "the plumbers". Mario and Luigi are clear favorites outside of Brooklyn and the Mushroom Kingdom. Luxury Prison Suite : For Bowser, his birdcage prison has been upgraded to what looks like a toy playset version of his castle fortress, complete with some amenities like scaled-down painting supplies. Mama Bear : Rosalina is shown to be very protective of her adopted children the Lumas, even willing to defend them when Bowser Jr. attacked. Meaningful Gift : Mario gifts Princess Peach her trademark parasol to utilize as a weapon and as a parachute to escape from harm. Medium-Shift Gag : During Fox's introduction, he explains his brief backstory in a more anime style animation in trying to hype himself up. And within that same backstory, the brief comment on blaming Slippy Toad for his warp-drive malfunction cuts to Slippy's profile from Star Fox 64 . Misplaced Retribution : Queen Bee quickly decides to punish Mario, Luigi, and Yoshi just because Peach's castle fell into her domain when really it was an unintentional accident. Regardless, she doesn't give the boys a chance to explain themselves and would've thrown all of them into the mines if Bowser hadn't volunteered to serve the punishment in the former three's place in exchange for letting them go. Moral Myopia : Before Mario and Peach are about to fight Bowser at Bowser Planet, Bowser angrily recalls how Peach "humiliated him at his wedding". Of course Bowser failed to mention that before Peach did that he invaded her kingdom and tortured one of her friends in order to force her to marry him. Morph Weapon : The first trailer and its extended version shows Bowser Jr. changing the tip of his Magic Paintbrush into different weapons, like a flail to throw at the Mario brothers, a spear, or an axe. Motifs : Skeletons are shown throughout the movie. The opening shot of the movie after the prologue is a Dry Bones run over by Mario and Luigi on their motorbikes. One of Bowser's paintings is a skeletal Mario. Yoshi flees New York City after destroying a dinosaur skeleton in a museum and runs on a dinosaur skeleton bridge in Fossil Forest. In Planet Bowser, a bunch of Koopas turn into Dry Bones after a dip in lava, the same with Bowser via Dry Bowser. Mundane Utility : Mario and Luigi using their fire forms via their Fire Flowers so their fireballs can be used as torches in the Inverted Pyramid. Kamek uses his wand as a airport/construction signal lighter. Mario uses the power of an Ice Flower to conjure an ice cream cone for a Toad. Musical Nod : An orchestral version of the Overworld theme from Super Mario World can be heard in the teaser as the camera flies through the kingdom. Another appearance of it is in the scene in the Yoshi First Look trailer where Mario is asking Yoshi to come out. At the end of the teaser, the opening notes of the main theme of Super Mario Galaxy play during the Title Drop . The little ditty Bowser hums to himself is in the same tune as his theme from Super Mario 64 . Rosalina's battle with Megaleg at the end of the first trailer is underscored by a rearrangement of the opening fanfare of the Gusty Garden Galaxy theme. When Mario and Luigi are riding their motorcycles on the way to Tostarena, the title screen music from the obscure Japan-only game "Famicom Grand Prix II: 3D Hot Rally" can be heard playing, as the game featured Mario and Luigi in promotional artwork. The scene of Frog Luigi rescuing a Toad is fittingly underscored by the Underwater music from Super Mario Bros. 3 . The scene where the UFO lasers the ground around Peach’s Castle and lifts it into space comes complete with the same theme that played when it happened in the original Galaxy game, along with Bowser Jr’s theme from that game as well when he first appears before Mario, Luigi and Yoshi. The hidden casino has a rendition of " Jump Up, Super Star! " playing. As tension builds with Wart, the score references the boss theme from Super Mario Bros. 2 before incorporating the final boss theme shortly after. When a tiny spaceship carrying Pikmin lands on the Gateway Galaxy, a small snippet of the "End of Day" theme from their home series plays. Fox's introduction is fittingly underscored by the main theme from Star Fox 64 , while the Arwing taking fire from Bowser Jr. is set to " Venom " (popularized as the Corneria stage music in Super Smash Bros. Melee ), and his piloting of the Comet Observatory features " Corneria " and the main theme of Star Fox 1 . The Cosmic Observatory approaching Planet Bowser in the final act is set to the theme for Bowser's Galaxy Reactor . The Ruined Dragon Bowser Jr. forms with his paintbrush comes complete with the same boss theme heard when fighting it. Daisy appearing in The Stinger is fittingly underscored by a snippet of the main theme of her debut game. My God, What Have I Done? : When Bowser falls to the lava and everyone assumes he perished, Mario and Peach look at each other in shock when they see Junior mourning him once it hits them that they may have rendered him an orphan. Mythology Gag : Still too many to count as in the previous film, so it has its own page here . During the casino fight, Birdo fires eggs at Peach while the latter uses her parasol as a shield. Birdo has been able to do this since its first appearance in Super Mario Bros. 2 , while Peach using her parasol has been a recurring ability in games like Super Princess Peach and Super Paper Mario , among other titles. Mouser and Clawgrip, also from Super Mario Bros. 2 , can also be seen in the scene. The chase for Ukiki in the Gateway Galaxy and its Underground is much like in the Yoshi's Island games and Super Mario 64 , where players chase them if they steal babies or caps. Another movie poster depicts Peach and Toad looking up at the sky. In other parts of it you can see Piantas , Star Bunnies , Whittles , and a Conkdor . There is also a planet in the background that heavily resembles the Gateway Galaxy serving as the very first planet in the first Galaxy game. Bowser Jr. pins Rosalina against a chair with a purple goopy Piranha Plant known as a Painted Piranha Plant when she tries to fight back on the under construction Planet Bowser. The reference here is twofold: both to the Proto Piranhas that Bowser Jr made with his brush in his debut game , and this particular Piranha Plant greatly resembles the Cloud Piranhas . The latter is especially appropriate, given Jr is in his Wonder appearance when he does this. Among the holographic signs in the Gateway Underground is the Nintendo 64 logo. Peach defeats Wart by throwing a vegetable into his mouth, just like how he is defeated in Super Mario Bros. 2 . After a failed attempt at reaching Bowser Jr.'s flying saucer, Fox's Arwing crash lands on a Dinosaur Planet . Even his mission to save the god-like Rosalina from being weaponized as a Planet Destroyer by Bowser Jr. is similar to how his old nemesis Andross weaponized the Krazoa Spirits to return to life as an Eldritch Abomination . The powered-up form Bowser Jr. takes on via his magic paintbrush has elements of both Bowser's "Fury" form from Bowser's Fury , and his own "Wonder" form from Super Mario Bros. Wonder . When Luigi suggests that they rebuild Peach's castle, Rosalina puts on a construction workers hat, much like E. Gadd, Luigi and the ghosts did when they prepared to rebuild The Last Resort hotel in Luigi's Mansion 3 . Negated Moment of Awesome : Rosalina's fight with Megaleg has her effortlessly counter its attacks, damage it repeatedly, and cripple its legs. But just as she's about to finish it off, one of her Lumas peeks out from the door, and Megaleg immediately fires on it, and by extension, the other Lumas. Forced into a Sadistic Choice , Rosalina immediately throws herself at the Bullet Bill, freezing it right before impact and throwing it away, but that leaves her wide open for Megaleg to strike her from behind and capture her. When Bowser Jr. taunts the captive Rosalina by threatening the Lumas, she grows furious, escapes her restraints, and carves through his army only for him to stop her himself with magic paint and put her in a more secure prison. When Bowser Jr. constructs the Ruined Dragon out of anger after Mr. Game and Watch shatters Dry Bowser to pieces, reducing him to a skull and dissembled pieces of bones, he orders it to attack Mario and co. only for it to attack him instead and eat him, forcing Mario to use the Red Star power-up to save him. Never My Fault : Though he makes an actually genuine effort to show that he's a changed Koopa, Bowser seems to have convinced himself that Peach broke his heart and humiliated him by leaving him at the altar , completely ignoring the fact that she never loved him at all and that he forced her into marriage by threatening to kill her people. Never Say "Die" : Not only completely Averted like with the previous movie, but in one case Inverted with someone saying "died" when "destroyed" would be the appropriate word choice. When Peach asked what happened to her castle at the end of the movie, Luigi nervously says "It... died." Never Trust a Trailer : The trailer that introduces Rosalina presents her as a badass who effortlessly annihilates Megaleg without a sweat (even showing it explode behind her). In the actual movie, Megaleg (which is piloted by Bowser Jr and Kamek) does give Rosalina a little trouble at first, but she initially has the upper hand in combat. What the trailers don't show is that it withstands the explosion and uses the opportunity of a Luma peeking out the door to kidnap Rosalina. Nice Job Fixing It, Villain! : When Mario and Peach reach Bowser on a bridge suspended over lava, Bowser throws an axe at them. Later, Mario pulls it out, throws it at Bowser, making him jump over it allowing Mario and Peach to run under and past him. Mario then uses the axe to shop the chains supporting the bridge making Bowser fall into the lava. However, this also works against them when Dry Bowser comes out. During the fight between Mario, Luigi, Yoshi, and the Bowsers, Dry Bowser punches Yoshi while the green (or rather, blue) dinosaur is currently in blimp fruit mode. Yoshi zooms all around and flies into Junior knocking the paintbrush out of the Koopa Prince's hands allowing Luigi to paint Mr. Game & Watch and defeat Dry Bowser. After retrieving his paintbrush, Junior paints the Ruined Dragon, only for it to go after him first . Noodle Incident : In Yoshi's flashback to his time spent in Brooklyn, he briefly sees Donkey Kong swinging through girders in the city while being chased by helicopters for reasons unknown. At Peach's birthday celebration, Mario tells her that Luigi cried at his tenth birthday party because he thought that his childhood was over. This event is not shown onscreen, so we'll have to take Mario's word for it. Bowser Jr. says he got expelled from his boarding school, but doesn't elaborate on why. Nothing Is Scarier : The unknown evil entity/forces that attacked Peach and Rosalina as children and tried to use their combined power for nefarious purposes are not shown at all onscreen and that is actively done on purpose to give their presence more of an eerie mystery as to what the heck they actually are and why they want Peach and Rosalina's combined power. Not Quite Dead : When Bowser attacks Mario and Peach on a bridge, Mario causes the bridge to collapse, leaving Bowser to fall into the lava where Bowser Jr. could only cry out, believing his father to be dead with Mario and Peach thinking the same. Bowser comes back as Dry Bowser not long after, ready to continue the fight with his son . Object-Tracking Shot : The teaser trailer follows the journey of a butterfly, which takes off from a napping Mario's hat, flies across the Mushroom Kingdom up to Princess Peach's castle, and into the sky before finally landing on the movie's logo above the clouds. Offscreen Karma : The last shot of Bowser and Bowser Jr. has them trying to pick up the pieces of Bowser's skeleton to reassemble him, with the heroes having seemingly forgotten about them. Then The Stinger reveals they were captured off-screen and thrown in jail. they’re going to escape the prison, but it might take them a while with that prison guard Lumalee, and it would take Bowser a long time to get back to normal. Ominous Crack : The Koopa Troop's UFO using lasers to cut Peach's Castle out of the ground shows the iconic stained glass window portrait of Peach starting to crack, before her face shatters and then things get worse. One-Man Army : Peach practically decimates all of Wart's forces, including Birdo and Mouser, which some assistance from Toad. Even his army of Ninjis barely slows her down and she has virtually no trouble taking care of Wart personally and forcing him to cough up Rosalina's location. Once More, with Clarity : The film begins when Rosalina begins reading a bedtime story about Princess Peach, before the Lumas ask her to talk about the Mario Brothers again and Bowser Jr. arrives at her doorstep. Later, when the Lumas pick up Mario, Peach, and their allies, Peach finds and reads the same story book. Leading to a flashback of the first film, it reveals that Rosalina and Peach are sisters with cosmic powers, but Rosalina had to send her away when an unknown force comes after them. The book ends with hopes that Peach would grow up to protect others, leading to her being the ruler of the Mushroom Kingdom. The Oner : Nearly the entire teaser is a continuous shot, following a butterfly soaring over a sleeping Mario, the Mushroom Kingdom, and Peach's Castle before ending in the sky above. "On Patrol" Montage : After Peach departs into space to find and rescue Rosalina, a brief montage of Mario, Luigi and Yoshi protecting the Mushroom Kingdom follows. Out of Focus : While still a major character, Bowser's appearance is slightly downplayed in lieu of Bowser Jr. He doesn't even appear in the proper movie until nearly a third in. Overly Long Gag : Peach and Toad ask R.O.B. where they can find pilots at the Gateway Galaxy, but his movements when pointing towards the map monitor goes very slowly, doing it enough times with Peach demanding a quicker answer. Mario, Luigi and Yoshi later end up experiencing the same issue. Over-the-Top Roller Coaster : At Planet Bowser, several Koopa Troopas are shown riding a roller that immediately plunges into a Lava Pit , shortly before all of them emerge out of it as Dry Bones. Parasol of Pain : A clip reveals Peach receiving a parasol from Mario as a birthday gift, and trailers show her using said parasol to fight Ninjis and defend against Birdo's eggs. Planet Destroyer : Key to Bowser Jr.'s scheme is a weapon on Planet Bowser designed to destroy worlds, using Rosalina's powers as the fuel. As Peach and Rosalina managed to invoke their cosmic powers to terraform Planet Bowser before the weapon was fully active, it isn't known if the weapon truly can destroy worlds. Post-Victory Collapse : After a long hard day of traveling all over the Mushroom Kingdom helping anyone who needs help, the Mario Bros. and Yoshi can only lie down on the floor completely exhausted. Mario and Luigi don't even have the energy to do their usual hand bump. Power Trio : Mario, Luigi and Yoshi become this after the Bros find Yoshi in the Inverted Pyramid. During the "On Patrol" Montage , they’re seen rescuing Toads and stopping a Chain Chomp from terrorising Toad Town, and later work together to find Peach and Rosalina and defeat Bowser and Bowser Jr. Pragmatic Adaptation : While it's based on Super Mario Galaxy , the movie is less a retelling of that game's story and more an original Mario movie that reuses the "Mario in space" idea and reimagines characters and concepts from Galaxy to tell its own story, not unlike the first movie. Given how long Galaxy is (being 13-15 hours without 100% completion), on top of the film having to juggle other elements not from Galaxy , like the introduction of Yoshi and Bowser Jr., this is an understandable decision. Rosalina's backstory from the game is replaced with a simpler backstory that explains her and Peach to be sisters made from stardust . Likely because Rosalina's original backstory had little plot relevance (even in the Galaxy game, it was an optional Lore Codex that was isolated from the main plot) and would've clashed too much with the film's more lighthearted and comedic tone compared to the game (since part of it deals with Rosalina grieving the death of her mother ). The new backstory, while different, is more in line with the films tonally, gives Rosalina a stronger connection to one of the characters right off the bat, allows Peach to have a more significant role in the story, and ties back to the first film by building upon Peach's backstory from that film . Because of Rosalina's new backstory, the night of the Star Festival would not align well, so it is replaced with a celebration of Peach's arrival in the Mushroom Kingdom. Had the Arwing been a single-seat Space Fighter like in the original games, Fox's role in the movie — providing passage to Space Junk Galaxy — would have been more difficult to justify. As such, the movie version of the Arwing instead fits six. In several Mario games (e.g., Super Mario World , New Super Mario Bros. U , etc), Yoshi is usually born as a baby before growing to his adult size (usually by eating fruit). Since Yoshi has to be established early on, however, the film skips Baby Yoshi in favor of him being born already grown. Since the Comet Observatory is Demoted to Extra here, the Gateway Galaxy is made a spaceport here instead of a galaxy connected to the observatory. It also helps provide an explanation as to how locales like Fossil Falls and characters like Fox, the 8 bits, and Daisy can appear in the movie setting. It also allows Princess Daisy to appear without creating a Continuity Snarl with the first film, since Peach's backstory in that film establishes that she was the only human-ish character on the Mushroom Kingdom planet until Mario and Luigi's arrival . Redemption Rejection : Despite genuinely growing fond of Mario and Luigi, Bowser ultimately decides to go back to his villainous ways. Mario sadly acknowledges that it didn't have to be this way, to which Bowser responds by attacking him. Reformed, but Not Tamed : Downplayed. Bowser willingly helps the Bros once he's back to normal, even letting himself be imprisoned by the Queen Bee in exchange for their freedom; however, his aggressive nature remains. Once he reunites with his son, he's is hesitant about turning on the brothers. Unfortunately, Jr's influence sets him back to villainy, undoing the "reformed" part of this trope. Related in the Adaptation : Peach finds out in the middle of the film that Rosalina, another princess that she sets out to rescue, is her older sister in this continuity . Returning Big Bad : After Bowser Jr. convinces Bowser to return to villainy, he later appears on Planet Bowser back to his normal size, declaring his return to the Koopa Army with Bowser Jr. by his side. This was due to Mario giving in to his anger and hitting him on Honeyhive Galaxy, though he initially stays friendly with the Mario Bros and Yoshi until his son and his army convince him to go back to his villainous ways. The Reveal : It is revealed midway through the film that Princess Peach is the younger sister of Rosalina, who sent her to the Mushroom Kingdom to prevent an Unseen Evil from stealing their cosmic power. Save the Princess : The movie is predicated on saving Rosalina, who Bowser Jr. kidnaps to steal her cosmic powers. Save the Villain : The climax has Bowser Jr. get Swallowed Whole by his own paint-made Ruined Dragon, much to Dry Bowser's horror. Fortunately, Mario is able to power up with a Red Star, using his flight to needle-drive through the dragon's stomach and rescue Bowser Jr. from being eaten . "The Scream" Parody : Bowser paints a screaming skeletal Mario in his castle dollhouse. Screams Like a Little Girl : Mario and Luigi's reaction when they see Yoshi's glowing green eyes in the green pipe. Sequel Escalation : By virtue of being based on Super Mario Galaxy . The first movie was set on Earth, even if it was in another dimension. This one moves the action out into space and goes into more diverse locations and galaxies, and the action sequences are way more explosive than the first movie was. Sequel Hook : Fox gets some heavy focus in his final scene as he jets off to return to his adventures in space, with Toad loudly exclaiming aloud that he wants to see what comes next for him. When will Mario eventually work up the courage to finally ask Peach out on a date after she kissed him on the cheek in the end after her castle is rebuilt is left up in the air. Who or what originally tried to kidnap the princesses for their cosmic power is left open. Princess Daisy shows up at Gateway Galaxy in the second stinger, setting her up as a major player for a future sequel . Sequel Non-Entity : Save a brief non-speaking cameo from Donkey Kong, the Kongs are completely absent from the film. The Mario Bros.' family likewise is nowhere to be seen, although it's justified since the film takes place outside of Brooklyn. Shaking the Rump : After crashing into the Honeyhive Galaxy, a tiny Bowser, realising he’s free, celebrates and shakes his rear at Mario before trying to escape. Bowser: Bye, suckers!!! She Is Not My Girlfriend : Mario protests that he is "not in love with Peach" , after Luigi asked him if he has feelings for her. Shipper on Deck : Luigi and Yoshi enjoy teasing Mario about his interest towards Princess Peach but also try encouraging him at the same time to reveal his feelings to her. Implied with Fox. While flying them to the Space Junk Galaxy, he exclaims that he likes Mario while glancing between him and Peach. The Toads react with joy when Peach kisses Mario on the cheek at the end of the film when her castle is repaired. Ship Tease : The film makes it explicitly clear that Mario and Peach have grown closer than they were in the previous film, with Mario gifting Peach her iconic parasol, and Peach having entrusted Mario to watch over her kingdom while she goes out to save Rosalina. The climax of the film has them holding hands more often, and by the end of the film, it reached the point where Peach kisses Mario on the cheek after helping rebuild her castle. Shot-for-Shot Remake : When Dry Bowser rises out of the lava, his movements and the camera angles match exactly with the scene of the first movie when he rises from the rubble of his castle in Brooklyn. Shout-Out : During the scene where Bowser Jr. introduces himself to Mario and Luigi during the first official trailer, a brief but familiar sounding Scare Chord can be heard while Bowser Jr. transforms into his Wonder form. When teasing Mario about his feelings for Peach, Luigi burst into the song "That's amore", sung by Dean Martin in The Caddy . The closeup of the Tyrannosaurus opening its eye is similar to the scene in The Land Before Time where Cera accidentally wakes up Sharptooth. Another reference occurs when the Tyrannosaurus , after chasing Yoshi and Toad to a skeletal bridge, falls into a lake below, similar to how Sharptooth met his end, although the Tyrannosaurus in this movie survived. Also during that sequence, Yoshi and co. are chased through a bridge made out of a colossal dead animal's skeleton, resembling a similar scene from Godzilla x Kong: The New Empire . For bonus points, Kong crossed it while being followed by a baby, while Yoshi has to run through it while carrying multiple babies. In Planet Bowser, one of the functions of the rollercoasters is to dip the Koopa Troopa customers in lava, turning them into Dem Bones ... er, Dry Bones. The way the scene is shot, and the very premise, greatly resembles a late-game cutscene from The Curse of Monkey Island , where LeChuck 's pirates are Stripped to the Bone and reanimated through the same process. The one difference is that in the latter case, the pirates were shrieking as it all went down, whereas the Koopa Troopas cheer the whole experience . Peach and Rosalina’s backstory involves them living on a small planetoid, with one shot in particular serving as a homage to The Little Prince . Small Role, Big Impact : The Lumas. Just as Rosalina is about to lay the finishing blow on the Megaleg, a Purple Luma exits the library and mumbles having a nightmare, giving the Megaleg enough time to attack them and forces Rosalina to use her power to protect the Lumas. Later, the very same Lumas pick up on Luigi's signal, driving the Cosmic Observatory to Fossil Falls. Space Episode : Considering the title says "Galaxy", it was a given that the action was going to move out into space. Squishy Wizard : Like how he was incapacitated by a punch from Peach in the first movie, Kamek gets neutralised by way of being swallowed and turned into an egg by Yoshi when he tries to help Bowser Jr. with Megaleg, though he quickly recovers. Stealth Pun : Rosalina's castle/spaceship Comet Observatory is crewed by babyish, star-like Luminas. It's a Stellar Nursery! Bowser Jr. manages to strand the Arwing on a planet that is home to a Tyrannosaurus Rex. A " Dinosaur Planet " so to speak. When Mario cuts the chains on the bridge and sends Bowser falling to his supposed doom in the lava, their Friendly Enemies dynamic ends. Mario literally and figuratively burned a bridge. The Stinger : Two of them, before and after the credits. The first one has Fox McCloud returning to his home universe right after dropping off both Bowser and Bowser Jr. in a prison, where they are tormented by their warden, Lumalee. The second one after the credits has Ukiki steal a Whittle’s baggage, only to be knocked out by a familiar looking princess from Sarasaland… Stock Sound Effect : The Tyrannosaurus from Super Mario Odyssey uses the same roar as its Jurassic Park counterpart in the second trailer. Justified if one considers the fact that Universal owns both movies. The various noises baby Mario and Luigi make are sourced from stock audio. Story-Breaker Power : Rosalina is viewed by Bowser Jr. as "the most powerful princess in the universe" and she demonstrates being borderline unstoppable at the start of the film. He manages to capture her when he gets some of the Lumas involved and she is held captive until the end of film when Peach saves her. Sweet and Sour Grapes : Fox is trapped in Mario's universe due to his Arwing's warp drive malfunctioning and tearing a reality hole in the space-time continuum. When Peach hires him to help transport her and her friends, his Arwing becomes seemingly damaged beyond repair. But Fox still decides to stick with and aid the heroes, and he proves instrumental in defeating Bowser and Bowser Jr. in the film's climax. Rosalina rewards Fox's help by fixing the Arwing's warp drive, allowing him to fly back home to the Lylat System. Sympathy for the Devil : Bowser's captors moved him from the tiny and full enclosed bird cage to a bigger and better mini-castle with art supplies and plants. And Luigi compliments Bowser's painting, but with Mario, this is averted . Take That! : One invoked online promo (login required) where Bowser recaps the events of the first movie has him briefly rant about how his song "Peaches" lost the nomination for the Academy Award for Best Original Song to "I’m Just Ken" from Barbie (2023) . Bowser: I should've got an Oscar nom. I'll get you, Gosling . During his flashback, Fox McCloud blames Slippy Toad for ending up in another universe in a clear jab at the latter's status as an Annoying Video Game Helper (particularly in Star Fox 64 ). Terrifying Tyrannosaur : Yoshi, Toad and the Mario Brothers (as babies from getting hit by Bowser Jr.'s Super Scope) encounter a Tyrannosaurus rex that roars directly in their faces which causes both Yoshi and Toad to run for their lives. There Is Another : You thought Peach and Rosalina are the only princesses in the whole galaxy? In the post credit scene, turns out there's also another princess, Princess Daisy, who appears on the Gateway Galaxy, punching Ukiki after he stole a Whittle's luggage. Thwarted Escape : Rosalina attempts to escape Bowser Jr. after initially being captured, but he uses his Magic Paintbrush to cover her in paint via a Painted Piranha Plant, immobilizing her and putting her into a much stronger cage. Title: The Adaptation : The title, The Super Mario Galaxy Movie , indicates that this film will primarily adapt the two Super Mario Galaxy games. Took a Level in Badass : Both Mario and Luigi have improved their fighting capability since the events of the first film. Not only are they considered reliable by numerous characters in the film, the two can go toe-to-toe against Bowser and his son, Bowser Jr., during the final battle with greater ease compared to their first fight with Bowser. Toxic Family Influence : Goes both ways with Bowser and Bowser Jr. As expected, Bowser's evil nature led Jr. to going down the same path. But after Bowser grows genuinely fond of Mario and Luigi, actively choosing to help them and not wanting them hurt, it's Jr. that pulls Bowser back to the side of evil by repeating the villainous lessons Bowser himself taught him. Trailers Always Spoil : The final trailer has a shot of Bowser at full size greeting his army, spoiling that he will be freed at some point before the film even released. The final trailer also shows Lumalee returning as a prison warden , something that doesn't appear in the movie until The Stinger . Trapped in Another World : How the film explains Fox's presence. He was on a mission with the rest of Star Fox when an explosion transported him to Mario's world. In The Stinger , he reveals that Rosalina fixed his warp drive, and he goes back to his home universe. Trash the Set : Princess Peach's Castle gets completely wrecked after Bowser Jr. abducts it. Aside from the aforementioned abduction already causing damages to it, the Megaleg controlled by Kamek appears inside it, wrecking it even more. Then when Bowser Jr.'s UFO accidentally drops it, it crashes down upon the Honeyhive Galaxy where it first slides down a slope before falling over a ledge, where it becomes nothing more than a pile of debris. At the end of the film, Princess Peach can only stare when, after trying to show Rosalina and her children her castle, she discovers her home is gone. Thankfully, everybody pitches in to build a new castle. Truer to the Text : In modern games recreating the original Bowser fight from Super Mario Bros. , the axe that destroys the draw bridge is changed to either a button or a massive hinged axe to sidestep the implications of Mario using a bladed weapon that the original implied. Here, when facing Bowser, Mario grabs an axe Bowser earlier threw at him and uses it to directly destroy the bridge by taking out the supports. Turned Against Their Masters : Late in the movie, Bowser Jr. gets so pissed at Mario and Luigi for "killing" his dad that he creates a giant dragon in a fit of vengeance to fight the brothers. Said dragon almost instantly turns on him and eats him instead, forcing Mario to save Bowser Jr. from his own creation . Turn the Other Cheek : Averted with Mario, who shows Bowser utter contempt, with Bowser showing the same sentiment towards the plumber in red. However, there is a two-way example with Luigi and Bowser, who interact quite cordially considering: Bowser tore off part of his mustache when they first met, later tried to dunk Luigi in a lava pit as a wedding tradition and then tried to incinerate Mario to stop him getting the Super Star. Luigi ruined any chance Bowser had of winning by getting the Super Star with Mario and leaving the Koopa King with no power, fortress, or army. Unflinching Walk : Rosalina casually walks away from Megaleg after blowing it up, hands clasped in the front like a regal pose. The Unintelligible : Yoshi attempts to explain how he got into the pipe in the Sand Kingdom and his misadventures in Brooklyn to Mario and Luigi, but it ends up falling on deaf ears due him being only able to say his own name . Unseen Evil : Rosalina's storybook, which Peach discovers during her time at the Comet Observatory, reveals that the two sisters were separated because an unknown evil entity tried to kidnap them for their cosmic powers. This is why Rosalina sent Peach to the Mushroom Kingdom, where she eventually became its monarch after being raised by the Toads. Unusually Uninteresting Sight : Yoshi's recollection of his time in New York shows that he was treated like any other tourist in spite of being a cartoon dinosaur, right up until he caused chaos in a museum and had to flee. Presumably, New Yorkers have gotten used to strange visitors from the Mushroom Kingdom. Also, they're New Yorkers . Villain Decay : Deconstructed . Bowser goes from an unrepentant (though comedic) villain to a friendly turtle with anger issues who's reluctant to harm his new friends. Bowser Jr. sees this as sign of " corruption ", and thus takes Bowser on a little tour of Planet Bowser to remind him of who he once was and always meant to be, leading Bowser to fully return to being the genuinely threatening and mercilessly evil tyrant that he was in the first movie. Villain Forgot to Level Grind : Justified, given that Bowser spent some time in prison and the Mario Bros. took several leaps in the power scale after the first movie. By the time of the final battle, Mario, Luigi, and Yoshi are able to counter Dry Bowser and a transformed Bowser Jr. Wardens Are Evil : Lumalee appears in the first stinger as the prison warden for Bowser and Bowser Jr., and he certainly takes delight in freaking them out with his creepy, nihilistic ranting. We Hardly Knew Ye : Mr. Game & Watch has less than a minute of screen time before he is dispatched and killed by Bowser Jr., and is never mentioned again afterwards . Wham Shot : When Peach sees Rosalina's image in Gateway Galaxy, she flashes back to her as a baby and a teenage Rosalina letting go of her hand . What Happened to the Mouse? : Kamek vanishes shortly after Mario and company arrive on Planet Bowser. He isn't even seen in prison with Bowser and Jr. in the Mid Credits Scene . Wart is never mentioned again after being seen on a screen in Bowser Junior's UFO, fulfilling his word to warn him. The Bee Queen and her prisoners don't appear again after Bowser is taken by Bowser Jr. UFO. The alien Tyrannosaurus that was turned into a baby is seen briefly at the site of the crashed Arwing and is never seen again. It's also unknown if the cast transformed it back to normal or not. Wormsign : As to be expected whenever a Monty Mole appears. The Mole in the teaser burrows and leaves a visible trail as it attempts to outrun the butterfly — at least up until the moment the Mole accidentally crashes head first into a boulder. Wounded Gazelle Gambit : Ukiki pretends to be lost and alone so Toad will lower his guard, allowing the monkey to steal his backpack. He later tries to do the same thing with a Whittle in the second stinger , only to get punched out by Princess Daisy herself. You Can't Thwart Stage One : The ending of the second act sees Bowser at full size greeting his army to say he's returned. Turns out he reverted to normal size after Mario stomped on him after the Koopa King provoked him by calling him a failure and Bowser Jr. later returning him to the Koopa Troop via a tractor beam. You Have GOT to Be Kidding Me! : The Lumas react incredulously when Mario and Luigi reveal that Peach's Castle has been completely destroyed. Luma: What are you talking about?! "Hello, maggots! Trying to escape?" Accent Adaptation : Whittles speak with a You No Take Candle speech pattern in Galaxy 2 , but in this movie, they speak with Southern accents with more complex grammar. Accessory-Wearing Cartoon Animal : During Yoshi's brief adventure in Brooklyn, he’s wearing a baseball cap and sunglasses before losing them upon arriving in the Mushroom Kingdom. A Monty Mole in a baseball cap can be glimpsed in the Gateway Galaxy. During Yoshi's brief adventure in Brooklyn, he’s wearing a baseball cap and sunglasses before losing them upon arriving in the Mushroom Kingdom. A Monty Mole in a baseball cap can be glimpsed in the Gateway Galaxy. Actionized Sequel : There's more intense action scenes, bigger set-pieces and higher stakes compared to its predecessor, as Bowser Jr.'s plans threaten the universe. Actor Allusion : In the Nintendo Direct revealing the first trailer and cast, right before announcing that Benny Safdie is the voice of Bowser Jr., Jack Black describes him as an " uncut gem ". It's probably not a coincidence that the Honey Queen's punishment for Bowser was hard labor via breaking down boulders with a pickaxe. Wart, voiced by Luis Guzmán , is the boss of a casino that Peach and Toad visit when trying to catch the Ukiki that stole his backpack and shows his vicious side by releasing an army of Ninjis to fight them, as well as informing the Koopa Troop about Peach, much like José Gonzalo Rodríguez Gacha's temper during Narcos , who was played by Guzman during Season 1 of the show. Glen Powell , who had previously starred in Top Gun: Maverick , plays Nintendo's own premiere Ace Pilot Fox McCloud . Glen Powell , who had previously starred in Top Gun: Maverick , plays Nintendo's own premiere Ace Pilot Fox McCloud . Adaptational Badass : Zig-Zagged . While Rosalina isn't at all weak in the games, she was rarely confrontational. Only offering Mario her assistance in rescuing Peach and stepping in directly when situations actually required her power. Here, when Megaleg shows up on her doorstep, she's able to handle it with relative ease, only being captured when the Lumas got caught up. And even after this, she was able to break out of her restraints to protect the Luma captured alongside her, only being restrained once again because she was caught off-guard in the ensuing fight. Bowser Jr. was more a troublemaker in the games and he hardly ever took on Mario and Luigi without Bowser (if he wasn't asking for their help) or at least without his Junior Clown Car. In the film, thanks to the magic paint brush he carries and a ton of other weapons, he captures Rosalina to start and foils her escape attempt, confronts the brothers head-on to rescue his father, and even almost takes out Mario, forcing Luigi to jump in to save him. He was also the one to reform Bowser's army, create an entire planet to serve as their new home base, and build a doomsday weapon with the power to destroy the universe. The Frog Suit in Super Mario Bros. 3 , while it allowed the player to swim ridiculously fast underwater, was useless on land. In the movie, when Luigi acquires it, he not only jumps ridiculously high, but performs a powerful stomp: one strong enough to squash a Big Cheep Cheep. Yoshi stops a rampaging Chain Chomp in the "On Patrol" Montage by eating it, a feat typically impossible in the games. He is also able to outrun the falling Peach's Castle with Mario and Luigi ( two grown men ) on his back. The Cloud Flower powerup in Super Mario Galaxy 2 is limited for creating cloud platforms. In the movie, Luigi uses the clouds in combat against the Bowsers. He also gets to create far more clouds than the game’s limit of three. The Comet Observatory can fly through space under its own power. The Power Stars that were required for this in the original game have been Adapted Out . Dry Bowser in the games was finished off by being dropped hard enough to separate his bones. Here, that just results in a Losing Your Head -ish scenario. The Boomsday Machine in Super Mario Galaxy 2 was a multitiered tank which served as Bowser Jr’s last resort, but seemed incapable of any kind of mass destruction. In the movie, it drains Rosalina’s power and is stated to be capable of wiping out planets . Zig-Zagged . While Rosalina isn't at all weak in the games, she was rarely confrontational. Adaptational Context Change : In the original games, Bowser Jr. is very much a young child in both his voice and mannerisms. Here, Benny Safdie's voice performance and various bits of character dialogue imply that his movie-self is a teenager . Bowser Jr.'s Wonder transformation was originally triggered by Bowser's Wonder energy in Super Mario Bros. Wonder . Here, it's the result of Junior coating himself in paint from his Magic Paintbrush. The Galaxy games take place during the Star Festival, an event where a special comet passes over the Mushroom Kingdom skies once a century, bringing about a shower of star bits. Although there is a similar festival in the movie (complete with a star shower), it’s actually for Peach’s birthday — or, more accurately, the anniversary of when she was found by the Toads — with the special comet being Adapted Out . In the first Galaxy game, the UFO was commanded by Bowser in order to seize Princess Peach with her castle. Here, it is Bowser Jr. commanding the UFO to seize the castle in order to rescue his father. Furthermore, Bowser used airships to lift the castle with chains, while Bowser Jr. used the UFO's tractor beam to lift the castle. In Odyssey , the Cascade Kingdom is an island on the same planet as the Mushroom Kingdom that just so happens to be populated with realistic dinosaurs. Here, it is a separate planet. Mr. Game and Watch, instead of being a digital construct that his original games and Super Smash Bros. ran with the idea of, is recontextualized in this movie as a painting brought to life by Luigi attempting to use Bowser Jr.'s paintbrush, with him claiming the minimalist design is due to a lack of actual artistry skills. Much like the realistic dinosaurs in Odyssey , the Lord of Lightning was a non-cartoony dragon who was recruited by Bowser to stall Mario and is native to the Ruined Kingdom on the same planet as the Mushroom Kingdom. Here, The Lord of Lightning is a paint construct created by Junior's Magic Paintbrush as a Hail Mary against Mario, Luigi and Yoshi and his more intense design is implied to be Junior overcompensating for how Luigi took out Dry Bowser with his simple doodle that became Mr. Game and Watch. Adaptational Early Appearance : The form that Bowser Jr. takes when trying to free his father in Peach's Castle is based off his transformation from Super Mario Bros. Wonder , which was released 16 years after Galaxy . Adaptational Expansion : In Galaxy , the Gateway Galaxy was a simple cluster of small planetoids with nothing more than a few cottages and rocks with the main feature being a tower for observation. In the film, Gateway Galaxy is a bustling spaceport with a secret Vice City hidden underneath. Adaptational Explanation : It isn't explained why Bowser Jr didn't appear prior to Super Mario Sunshine in the games. Here, it's stated that he was at boarding school for years prior to the events of the film. In the original Super Mario Bros game, the axe on the bridge is a Deus ex Machina for gameplay purposes. Here, the axe has been combined with the hammers Bowser originally threw and Mario is able to grab a tossed axe to fell the bridge with Bowser on it. In the games, Peach's family is never shown or mentioned, with the only allusion to them in the series' entire history outside the occasional comic being the English instruction manual for the original Super Mario Bros. mentioning her father (the Mushroom King). Here, the film picks up the plot point of Peach's unknown origins from the first movie to reveal that Peach and Rosalina are blood sisters born and made from stardust, making them Ambiguously Human beings from space . In the games, it is never explained why Rosalina looks so much like Peach, with it ultimately just being The Artifact from an early development idea for them to be related. The final game still has Rosalina's mother look like a composite of the two and living in a castle that looks vaguely like Peach's, which combined with Rosalina living though universal resets has resulted in countless theories that one is the other's parent from an alternate timeline. In the movie canon, they are sisters, with Rosalina's own parents and brother being Adapted Out . It isn't explained why Bowser Jr didn't appear prior to Super Mario Sunshine in the games. In the movie, she tries to have him and Luigi imprisoned for ruining her flowers , and is rudely dismissive of them even after being convinced to help. She also treats her prisoners as slaves , whether they be Bowser or her own bee subjects. Adaptational Location Change : In the original Super Mario World , Mario met Yoshi on Yoshi's Island note Not the same Yoshi's Island from the first film based on Super Mario World 2: Yoshi's Island , but a separate location with the Japanese name of Yo'ster Island . In the movie, Mario and Luigi instead meet him in Tostarena’s Inverted Pyramid. In Super Mario Sunshine , Mario meets Bowser Jr for what's implied to the first time at Isle Delfino, while Luigi's first interaction with him implicitly occurs a short walking distance away from Peach's Castle in the opening cutscene of New Super Mario Bros. 1 . Here, both brothers meet him for the first time inside the castle. Adaptational Nice Guy : In the games, the Mandibugs were neighbors of the bees that became part of the Koopa Troop after becoming hostile towards them from the first Galaxy game onwards. Here, they're steads of the bees and one helps Mario, Luigi, and Yoshi go to the Gateway Galaxy after Bowser takes their place of working in the Honeyhive Galaxy's mines. Adaptational Ugliness : Compared to the Black Bead Eyes Bowser Jr. has in the games, the bloodshot sclerae around his pupils can be seen here, most likely to make him more expressive and look more threatening in the previews. This is downplayed in the movie proper, since his bloodshot sclerae are only visible in certain shots. Adaptational Villainy : While they tend to be a nuisance whenever they appear as enemies, the Ukikis from the games are at best Mischief Making Monkeys that get a kick out of annoying the protagonists. The Ukiki seen in this movie, however, is an actively malicious thief that manipulates people in order to steal their valuables. They also work for Wart and the 8-Bits, and it's implied that they deliberately lured Peach and Toad to Wart so he could try and kidnap her. Adaptational Wimp : Rosalina effortlessly destroys Megaleg in the opening fight, which is not only smaller than its game counterpart, which was the epitome of Colossus Climb , but puts up even less of a fight. Downplayed when Megaleg manages to survive the damage and remains functional enough to eventually overpower and capture Rosalina. Downplayed with Bowser Jr.'s Magic Paintbrush. While it does amplify his combat prowess significantly, and can still summon paint enemies, he never uses it to turn into Shadow Mario. In the games, Bowser’s Dry Bowser form was invincible and Mario would need outside help to take him down. In the movie, Bowser in said form can still be staggered and even shares the same weakness as Dry Bones if he gets enough hits. Adaptation Amalgamation : Though the film's central story is based on Super Mario Galaxy , Yoshi's biggest scenes take inspiration from Super Mario World and its prequel Super Mario World 2: Yoshi's Island , while locations and characters from Super Mario Bros. 2 and Super Mario Odyssey are also thrown in the mix. The climax features a dramatisised recreation of the original Bowser Fight from Super Mario Bros. 1 and Dry Bowser's resurrection from New Super Mario Bros. 1 . Adapted Out : Bowser Jr.'s Magic Paintbrush uses the design introduced in Super Nintendo World and Bowser's Fury , which bears his personal insignia instead of Professor E. Gadd 's like in Super Mario Sunshine . E. Gadd's insignia does appear on Kamek's coffee cup in the intro, however. Rosalina's backstory was changed so that she and Peach were born from stardust, meaning her mother, father, and brother from the original storybook don't exist. Lubba is absent, with her role in Galaxy 2 essentially given to Fox — a pilot whose ship needs repairs and then escorts the heroes, though in this case the Arwing instead of Starship Mario ). Fox also ends up taking part of Rosalina's role in the original Galaxy game as he pilots the Comet Observatory towards the final battle with Boswer, but stays on the ship rather than joining the action on foot. Advertised Extra : Despite receiving top-billing and being a major character in the original Super Mario Galaxy game, Rosalina doesn't play that much of an active role after her fight against Megaleg in the opening scene due to being captured by Bowser Jr. Birdo was promoted in promotional materials for the film and also made it seem like she was going to be one of the major characters, but in reality, she only has 21 seconds of screen time. Affectionate Gesture to the Head : Surprisingly, Bowser does this to Mario, even making his hat fall off, after Mario steps on him, reversing the effect of the blue mushroom and earning a big kiss from Bowser. Ageless Birthday Episode : Justified; after encountering Yoshi in Tostarena, the brothers attend Peach's birthday party in the Mushroom Kingdom. Her age not being mentioned is justified by Peach noting that the event isn't even her actual birthday, given no one has any clue of her origins, but rather the day the Toads found her in the Mushroom Kingdom. Alas, Poor Villain : While he might have reverted to being a full-on villain by this point, Bowser falling into the lava in his castle is played for drama, with Mario and Peach both horrified to have dealt him such a fate — and even more so when Bowser Jr. starts weeping over his father's demise. At least until he rises back up as Dry Bowser , at which point he and Jr. go back on the attack. Bowser accidentally shuts the door on his tail, prompting a scream of pain from him. Luigi's dramatic moment where he attacks Bowser Jr. with a knee strike is undercut by him whimpering in pain and rubbing his knee afterwards. Art Evolution : The film presents more detailed and polished backgrounds than in the previous film, which were already very high quality. The change also includes minor adjustments to the character designs. Art Shift : When Fox McCloud is introduced, his backstory with the Star Fox team and the explanation of how he entered Mario's universe is told in the style of a 90s American action cartoon. When turned into a baby, the T. rex goes from realistic to cartoony, complete with a diaper. Bowser Jr.'s gauntlet is controlled via a screen that renders his view of the action in the style of Super Mario Maker ' s Super Mario Bros. mode. The montage of building Peach a new castle at the end is depicted through spritework based on Super Mario World , with Luigi and Toad's sprites taken from Super Mario Maker 2 and Rosalina given all new sprites based on Peach's. Ascended Meme : Fox states during his introduction that one of the things he loves doing is "extreme dangerous barrel rolls", with Toad asking him to do one with the line "Do a barrel roll!" later on in the film. Bowser Jr's real-time obstacle course modification brings to life the joke that all the sadistic fan-made levels in Super Mario Maker were really made by Bowser or Bowser Jr. Fox states during his introduction that one of the things he loves doing is "extreme dangerous barrel rolls", with Toad asking him to do one with the line "Do a barrel roll!" later on in the film. Bowser Jr's real-time obstacle course modification brings to life the joke that all the sadistic fan-made levels in Super Mario Maker were really made by Bowser or Bowser Jr. Auto-Tune : R.O.B. speaks with a monotonous voice that often has abrupt pitch shifts several times. Justified, as he is a robot . Ass Kicks You : Peach was seen doing her signature Peach Bomber move from Super Smash Bros. , slamming her butt on one of the Ninjis which sends them flying. Baby Morph Episode : When Bowser Jr. hits the Mario Bros. with a Super Scope, they're turned into babies and have to be rescued from a hungry T. rex in Fossil Falls. Toad later gets turned into a baby during the subsequent chase scene and the T. rex is turned into one when the babies inadvertently use the Super Scope on it. Badass Biker : Invoked with Mario and Luigi, with the sequence of them riding across the Sand Kingdom on motorbikes. The brothers excitedly declare themselves as “Cool Motorcycle Guys” while congratulating each other. Badass in Distress : Powerful as she is, Rosalina spends most of the movie being held by Bowser Jr. She puts up a good fight in the opening, holding her own until her Lumas are threatened, but she was at no point going to win, if the ease with which Bowser Jr. defeats her the second time is any indication. This is due to most of her powers being dependent on her being able to channel magic through her wand, and Junior's magic paint is able to easily prevent this by coating her hands. Bad Guy Bar : Peach and Toad end up in one after chasing a Ukiki for Toad's backpack, finding a bar in the Gateway Underground filled with various Minions such as Goombas, Koopa Troopas, Hammer Bros, Sledge Bros, Octoombas, Octoguys, Chargin' Chucks, Shy Guys, Snifits and Coin Coffers of various colors, who glare at the duo when they enter. Bad Is Good and Good Is Bad : Things that would get any other child a harsh scolding from any reasonable parent, such as getting expelled from boarding school and denouncing friendship, just makes Bowser proud of his little boy. Bait-and-Switch : The teaser begins with a medley of tracks from Super Mario World as the camera pans throughout the Mushroom Kingdom, implying the movie will take cues from said game... before the camera pans all the way up to space to reveal the title, complete with the main theme from Super Mario Galaxy playing in the background. The bait-and-switch was added by a then-recent Universal press release containing release dates for their upcoming films called the film Super Mario World as a false title to hide the Galaxy connection, so Nintendo could reveal it themselves. Bowser's first scene shows him, seemingly restored, painting in his castle... only for a giant Luigi to appear in the window, revealing that Bowser is still shrunken down and imprisoned in Peach's Castle, albeit now in a replica of his own castle. During the "On Patrol" Montage scene, a Chain Chomp is rampaging through the streets of Toad Town, sending the panicking civilians running for their lives. Mario and Luigi stand in its path, posing triumphantly in preparation for the impending fight. Yoshi jumps between them and nips the threat in the bud by eating the Chain Chomp. The flabbergasted looks on the brothers' faces says it all. Once Mario is finally goaded into punching Bowser hard enough to undo the Mini Mushroom's effects, Bowser returns to full size clutching him tightly in one of his claws and staring at him menacingly, making it look like everything he's said about turning over a new leaf was just a lie and he's about to immediately attack him. Instead, he gives Mario a giant smooch on the cheek and promises he won't regret it, showing that he was sincere after all. Bowser ultimately does return to being evil later, but his attempt at redemption was Not an Act . When Peach first introduces the Bros and Yoshi to Fox, Mario and Fox have a long stare-down suggesting that they're going to form an immediate rivalry over Peach's affection. Instead, they genially exchange compliments (Mario likes Fox's ship and Fox likes Mario's mustache) and get along well the whole time, with Fox even commenting at one point, "I like this guy!" When Luigi gets his hands on Bowser Jr.'s paintbrush, it looks like he's about to summon a giant abomination to fight on the heroes' behalf. Instead he draws Mr. Game & Watch, with him stating that he never took art lessons. However , Mr. Game & Watch still has enough power to use Judgment to strike Dry Bowser into skeletal parts. Bash Brothers : After settling into the Mushroom Kingdom, Mario and Luigi are now its protectors. They’re shown to be helping Toads and Tostarenans, as well as carrying out their plumbing duties. Beware the Nice Ones : Luigi is shown to be somewhat friendly towards Bowser, and tries to greet Bowser Jr. and calm the situation down. When Bowser Jr. is dangerously close to killing Mario, Luigi stops him by kneeing him in the face. Big Bad : The entire story begins with Bowser Jr. taking his father's place and remaking the army into a far more fearsome force to the point where he created a miniature planet with a weapon capable of leveling the entire universe with the right power source. Once he properly rescues his father, quickly swaying him to return into his villainous ways , the two become the Big Bad Duumvirate of the movie that needs to be taken down. Big Brother Instinct : Considering that Mario struggles to even look at Bowser in their first scene together, it's clear that he still hasn't gotten over his nemesis nearly killing his brother. Inverted when Mario, Luigi and Yoshi fight Bowser Jr. The Koopa has Mario pinned on the floor, with the only thing keeping Mario from being chopped to pieces from the Koopa kid's paintbrush axe being a book holding the axe in place. Luigi then lunges in, kneecapping Bowser Jr. in the face. After the reveal that Peach and Rosalina are sisters , the latter plays it straight and the former inverts it, too. In the backstory, the teenage Rosalina sent Baby Peach away in a warp pipe to somewhere that she would be safe when villains tried to come after them for their combined power. And once Peach discovers they're related, she becomes even more determined in Storming the Castle with her friends to save her. Big Damn Reunion : Between Peach and Rosalina in the finale, especially after it is revealed they were long-lost sisters. Big "WHAT?!" : Mario lets out one when Luigi teases him on having romantic feelings for Princess Peach. Bizarrchitecture : Peach fights with Birdo, Mouser, and Clawgrip inside a casino that takes up the inside sides of a cube. Black Comedy : One of the amusement park rides on Planet Bowser is a rollercoaster that scorches Koopas with lava, instantly transforming them into Dry Bones. Surprisingly, they seem to be perfectly fine with this. Body Motifs : Hands. Mario attempts to hold hands with Peach after they reunite in the Gateway Galaxy, and later extends his hand to her when the two go through Bowser Jr's. gauntlet . Peach's flashback upon seeing a picture of Princess Rosalina is that of a younger Rosalina letting go of baby Peach's hands as she was sucked into a warp pipe. and both of their powers activate when their hands are pressed together. In the second stinger, Princess Daisy's first shown punching out the Ukiki . Bookends : One that calls back to the first film. The first introduction to the Mario Bros. shows their commercial, which has a tacked-on shot of Mario flying with a cape. This film concludes with Mario flying upward with an actual cape a la Super Mario World for its final shot. Borrowed Catchphrase : The teaser ends on a yellow Luma quoting Mario's famous "let's-a go" phrase. Another Luma then says it in the film proper itself before Mario and the gang head into Bowser’s planet. Brick Joke : In the aftermath of Bowser Jr.'s attempted capture of Peach's castle, it ends up being destroyed and left a pile of rubble in Honeyhive Kingdom . At the end of the film, when Rosalina and the Lumas agree to visit Princess Peach's home , the Mario brothers give each other a quick Oh, Crap! look. Smash cut to everyone staring at the large hole in the Mushroom Kingdom where the castle used to be. After voicing his support for Mario's affections for Peach, Luigi shyly mumbles that maybe she could help find somebody for him too. The final Stinger of the film does just that, introducing Princess Daisy. Brutal Honesty : Bowser shows Mario and Luigi his "masterpiece", a self-aggrandizing portrait of himself with a swooning Peach. While Luigi praises the use of colors , Mario doesn't even bother trying to sugarcoat how he feels and remarks that "it's trash". Call-Back : After Mario and Luigi meet Yoshi within Sand Kingdom's Inverted Pyramid, Mario tells the Tostarenans that they "found the problem," a call-back to the line he says in the first movie after the plumbing scene. Bowser's tiny castle has the same tiny piano from the first movie. Luigi's bad knees from the first movie get a nod with Luigi painfully rubbing one of them after kneeing Bowser Jr.'s Wonder Form in the face. When Mario meets Fox for the first time, he compliments the Arwing. As seen in the first film, Mario owns a model of the same ship. After Peach reunites with Rosalina in the climax, Mario and Luigi meet up with them, and Luigi states, "I love a family reunion," followed up by him and Mario saying "ayy" with an arms open pose that they've used in the first movie after their handshake. The Cameo : Donkey Kong and Foreman Spike make brief appearances in Brooklyn while Yoshi is explaining his time there. Doc Louis and Little Mac also appear as a Freeze-Frame Bonus . Some Pikmin appear at the Gateway Galaxy spaceport, in a Bait-and-Switch for an appearance of Olimar. The rest of Star Fox is shown in Fox's flashback. Luigi conjures up Mr. Game and Watch using Bowser Jr.'s paint brush. During the mid-credits scene, Lumalee makes a return as Bowser and Bowser Jr.'s jailer. In the post-credits scene, Princess Daisy punches out Ukiki before they can steal a tourist's bag. Canon Welding : While the first film subtly alluded to other Nintendo properties through Easter Eggs , this installment utilizes its galactic setting to integrate other franchises into the canon. Fox McCloud , hero of the Star Fox franchise, plays a major supporting role. He was transported to here from the Lylat System when his ship got damaged in a mission, and is currently seeking a way back home. A group of Pikmin is shown landing their ship at Gateway Galaxy. R.O.B. , the robotic peripheral from Nintendo's heyday (and a known Guest Fighter from both Mario Kart DS and Super Smash Bros. ) works at Gateway Galaxy as a desk attendant. Mr. Game & Watch appears as a living drawing, using sounds and moves from Super Smash Bros. . Though this isn't the first time he's been directly associated with the Mario brand, having a subseries of remakes in Game & Watch Gallery and background cameos in-person in games like Donkey Kong Country Returns . Casino Park : The casino that Peach and Toad chase the Ukiki into has a giant roulette wheel on one of its sides, and Peach at one point fights a horde of Ninji on it, even using the giant roulette ball to knock out some of the horde. Central Theme : Families are a recurring theme throughout the movie. Found family can be found throughout the good guys. Yoshi is immediately a part of the Mario gang without hesitation, Rosalina is the mother of the Lumas, and Princess Peach's birthday celebration is celebrating the day the Toads found her. Fox McCloud speaks of fondness of his crew, Star Fox, like family. Family influence. The Toads raised Princess Peach as one of their own, which she reciprocates. Mario only snaps and attacks the shrunken Bowser when Bowser calls him a "failure of a plumber", a reminder of his father's criticisms. Luigi acts like a wingman for Mario's feelings towards Princess Peach. Last because of how much of a Toxic Family Influence Bowser was on his son, Bowser Jr. manages to pull his dad back out of his redemption to conquer the universe. While riding a rollercoaster on Planet Bowser, some Koopas get doused in lava and get turned into Dry Bones. When Bowser himself falls in lava, he turns into Dry Bowser. Chekhov's Gun : Peach's parasol which Mario gave her as a gift for the Star Festival. It comes in handy during the fight in Wart's casino lair and later used as a way to get out of a trap Bowser Jr. sets for Mario and Peach. Chekhov's Skill : We see in a flashback that Peach and Rosalina touching hands can send out an energy wave. Peach was younger at the time, so it wasn't very powerful. In the present, when she and Rosalina do so just as the Bowser Jr.'s doomsday machine is powering up, it's enough to destroy it and preform a World-Healing Wave over the planet Bowser Jr. made as a base. Luigi's habit of rapidly throwing things. He first does it with some fireballs after getting spooked by a Buzzy Beetle, then cowardly chucks rocks to block the Warp Pipe containing Yoshi (whom he initially thinks is a monster). In the Final Battle , he barrages Dry Bowser with clouds in the same fashion, and this time, it's not out of cowardice.
